figures.gloss: The supreme creator and sky father who dwells in the highest heaven. He is the remote observer of the cosmos, controlling the weather, the seasons, and the over-arching fate of humanity, yet he usually delegates direct interaction to lesser spirits.
figures.facet: He is profoundly distant. Unlike strictly interventionist sky gods, Num is structurally withdrawn; he frequently loses cosmic wagers to his dark rival Nga, reflecting a harsh Arctic reality where life does not always triumph over death.
figures.provenance: M. A. Castren's 19th-century ethnographies; later corroborated by A. V. Golovnev.

figures.gloss: The lord of the underworld, darkness, and disease. Dwelling beneath the earth, he is an eternal, hungry force that consumes the life essence of humans and demands constant propitiation through reindeer blood.
figures.facet: He is not a 'fallen' being or a moral evil, but a necessary, co-eternal metabolic counterpart to Num. Cosmic equilibrium is maintained only through the constant negotiation and sacrificial feeding of Nga's endless hunger.
figures.provenance: Detailed in T. Lehtisalo's 'Entwurf einer Mythologie der Jurak-Samojeden'.

figures.gloss: 'Mother Earth', the great protector of life, women, and childbirth. She anchors the terrestrial realm, providing the vital lichen for the reindeer and safeguarding the vulnerable spaces of the nomadic camp.
figures.facet: Her power is mapped directly onto the architecture of the 'chum' (tent). She governs the strict spatial taboos separating 'clean' male spaces from 'unclean' female spaces, embedding cosmic law into everyday domestic geography.
figures.provenance: L. V. Khomich, 'Nenets'; A. V. Golovnev, 'Talking Cultures'.

figures.gloss: The ecstatic shamanic practitioners who mediate between the human camp and the spirit realms. They are strictly classified by their spirit alliances: the 'Vy'du'ana' serve Num, while the 'Ya'nyang' negotiate with Nga.
figures.facet: The tadibya's power is deeply tied to the drum and the reindeer. They do not merely trance; they orchestrate a literal metabolic exchange, trading the blood and breath of a sacrificial reindeer for the stolen soul of a sick human.
figures.provenance: T. Lehtisalo; mirrored in broader Siberian ethnographic records.

figures.gloss: The sacred sledge (khege) that carries the clan's spiritual effigies, ancestral figures, and ritual objects. It travels with the nomadic caravan, never ridden by humans, acting as the spiritual anchor of the community.
figures.facet: In an obligate nomadic culture, the temple cannot be a stationary building. The axis mundi itself must be mobile, a vehicle dragged across the permafrost by sacred reindeer, moving the center of the world wherever the herd goes.
figures.provenance: I. Krupnik, 'Arctic Adaptations'; A. V. Golovnev.

figures.gloss: A gigantic, supernatural thunder-bird of the heavens. It creates the fierce Arctic winds and blizzards by beating its massive iron wings, serving as a terrifying meteorological agent of Num.
figures.facet: Storms are not magical atmospheric phenomena but mechanical output. The sheer friction and physical violence of this creature's metallic feathers perfectly encapsulate the brutal, physical reality of the Siberian winter.
figures.provenance: Nenets epic songs (yarabts); recorded by M. A. Castren.

figures.gloss: The 'Old Woman of the Tent', a central hearth-guardian and faceless wooden effigy kept on the sacred sledge. She is brought inside the chum specifically to protect women during childbirth and menstruation.
figures.facet: While the male tadibya bargains with the vast, freezing cosmos, she represents a hyper-local, intimate animism. She embodies the sacredness of the enclosed, heated domestic space, defending the biological vulnerability of human reproduction.
figures.provenance: L. V. Khomich's studies on Nenets women's rituals.

figures.gloss: The supernatural master and protector of the wild reindeer. He oversees the vast herds of the tundra, dictating the success of the hunt and, by extension, the health of the domesticated reindeer.
figures.facet: In a culture entirely defined by reindeer pastoralism, he is the ultimate arbiter of survival. He embodies the fragile threshold between wild prey and domesticated wealth, capable of recalling the herds if disrespected.
figures.provenance: Core Nenets folklore; detailed in Soviet-era ethnographies of Siberian peoples.

figures.gloss: Roughly carved anthropomorphic wooden figures erected at sacred sites, fed with reindeer blood, fat, and other offerings — the standing, material bodies of the spirits at fixed holy places, distinct from the mobile effigies carried on the sacred sledge.
figures.facet: Where the sledge cult keeps the sacred mobile, the syadai are its opposite pole: spirits localized permanently in carved, fed wood at a named place, the fixed complement to a nomadic religion's traveling shrine.
figures.provenance: L. V. Khomich, 'Nenets'; T. Lehtisalo, 'Entwurf einer Mythologie der Jurak-Samojeden'.

figures.gloss: An effigy made for a deceased household member, classically a respected elder — dressed, ritually fed, and kept inside the chum for years after death, continuing that person's protective presence within the family.
figures.facet: Death does not remove the person from the household economy of care; it relocates them into an object that must still be dressed and fed, keeping ancestral authority materially present rather than merely remembered.
figures.provenance: L. V. Khomich, 'Nenets'; A. V. Golovnev, 'Talking Cultures'.

figures.gloss: Small people said to have retreated underground when the Nenets arrived in the tundra, herding 'earth reindeer' (identified by some tellers with mammoth remains eroding from the permafrost) and emerging mainly at night; encounters with them are read as omens, sometimes dangerous.
figures.facet: They are the Nenets' own account of a displaced prior people, and the legend runs directly into the physical record — mammoth tusks and bones surfacing from thawing ground are explained as evidence of the Sikhirtya's herds.
figures.provenance: A. V. Golovnev, 'Talking Cultures'; ethnographic notes on Sikhirtya tied to archaeological discussion of pre-Nenets tundra sites.

figures.gloss: 'Holy island,' Vaygach, between the Barents and Kara seas — its capes, above all Bolvansky Nos, held generations of accumulated syadai idols as the paramount pan-Nenets pilgrimage and offering site, until Russian Orthodox missionaries destroyed much of the idol accumulation in the 1820s; the cult persisted at reduced scale afterward.
figures.facet: Against a religion organized around the mobile sledge, this one island stands as the fixed exception — a single shared axis for a scattered, nomadic people, and therefore also the single point a hostile mission could target to try to break the cult at its root.
figures.provenance: I. Krupnik, 'Arctic Adaptations'; A. V. Golovnev, 'Talking Cultures'; 19th-century missionary accounts of the Vaygach idol destructions.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The eternal structural tension and cosmic wagers between the remote sky father and the hungry lord of death.
Intra-tradition bond: The macrocosmic mother of the earth paired with the microcosmic, effigy-bound mother of the domestic tent.
Intra-tradition bond: The sacred vehicle and the most vital feminine protective effigy it transports across the tundra.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The high sky and weather god shared broadly across Uralic and Finno-Ugric cosmological frameworks.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The vast, overarching blue sky deity of the Eurasian steppe and tundra.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A remote, atmospheric supreme power governing the severe weather of the Arctic.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Siberian lord of the underworld who demands sacrifices and stands in constant opposition to the creator god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A co-eternal principle of destruction and disease that structurally requires the creator's constant opposition.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The exact shared Siberian cosmological framework of navigating vertical realms to negotiate with spirit lords.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ecstatic Arctic specialists who enter deep trance to locate and retrieve lost human souls.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The colossal avian entity whose physical movements mechanically generate extreme weather.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, winged meteorological force that serves as a sky deity's power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme supernatural owner of the primary Arctic herd animal (reindeer/caribou).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Uralic woodland/tundra master who directly controls the hunter's luck and the movement of game.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fierce, immanent goddess of the physical earth and womb who balances the remote masculine sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose structural parallel: architectural constructs (a sledge vs. a granary) that physically house the cosmological order.
Independent Arctic and sub-Antarctic parallels: a living shamanic office empowered through dream and inherited spirit-power, worked for weather and healing.
The Vaygach sanctuary's capes were crowded with generations of these idols, per the sanctuary's own gloss.
Both are effigies kept within the domestic chum, tending different needs — ancestor continuity and childbirth protection respectively.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: constructed spirit-vessels fed with offerings to bind and localize spiritual power, in wholly unrelated traditions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: power objects built up and fed over time to house and concentrate spiritual force at a fixed site.
Structural analogy across separate Siberian cultures (Samoyedic and Mongolic): an effigy made to house and continue a specific deceased person's protective presence.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of the Nenets, a Samoyedic-speaking reindeer-herding people of the northwest Siberian Arctic (the Yamal, Gydan, and Taimyr tundra and adjacent forest). Animist and shamanic, it organizes a nomadic pastoral life around a remote sky-god, Num, a hungry underworld-god, Nga, an earth-mother, and a dense population of local and household spirits.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is vertically tiered — an upper sky ruled by the withdrawn creator Num, the middle earth personified as Ya-Nebya ('Mother Earth'), and an underworld of darkness and disease ruled by Nga, to whom sickness and death are owed and reindeer are sacrificed. The world is not a moral battlefield of good against evil but a balance kept by constant exchange: Num and Nga are complementary, and human life is sustained by feeding the spirits their due. Souls, illness, and fate are handled through negotiation with these powers rather than by any doctrine of reward and punishment after death.
dossier.practice: Worship is enacted through the blood sacrifice of reindeer at sacred sites (hills, islands, and groves marked with idols and antlers), household and clan idols carried on a dedicated sacred sledge that is never ridden by people, offerings of food and cloth, and strict spatial taboos inside the chum (tent) separating clean from unclean and male from female zones. Specialists, the tadʼebʼa (shamans), classed by which spirit-world they serve, drum into trance to retrieve stolen souls, escort the dead, and treat sickness. Everyday life — herding, the tent, childbirth guarded by the effigy Myad-pukhutsya — is saturated with ritual observance.
dossier.canon: An oral tradition of epic songs (syudbabts and yarabts), myths, and shamanic practice — no writing; it is preserved in ethnographies beginning with M. A. Castrén's 19th-century fieldwork and continuing through Lehtisalo, Khomich, and Golovnev.
dossier.reflects: Religion as survival economics in an extreme environment — the sacred grasped as a ledger of debts to be paid, where cosmic balance is literally fed rather than merely believed.
sources: Toivo Lehtisalo, 'Entwurf einer Mythologie der Jurak-Samojeden' (1924)
Andrei V. Golovnev & Gail Osherenko, 'Siberian Survival: The Nenets and Their Story' (1999)
